Quotation Content: San Pablo Bay supports these very extensive mudflats that serve as these reservoirs for sediment so you’ll get a big wet winter, a bunch of sediment will come out of the watersheds, it will accrete on those mudflats and then during the summer that sediment will get resuspended off of those mudflats by wind waves and advected into these subsided diked baylands on a flood tide, decant and then on an ebb tide that sediment will be left in the site. So we, and I think I talked about this a little bit in the site visit, a huge goal, design goal of [project] was to maximize accretion. Was to take advantage of that connectivity to this giant reservoir of sediment and get as much of that sediment into the site as possible to jumpstart elevation recovery, get that elevation capital back and establish tidal wetland vegetation and start that positive feedback loop of more vegetation, more sediment, more vegetation, more sediment.

So there's definitely one of those asymptotic curves about how fast we can actually even apply sediment. Which is why I think when they went out to [other project], at one point there was like foam in the levee. You know for every foot that you go up you actually have to go up 3 because it sinks one and a half. So there are many challenges to simply putting more, you know, smacking more stuff on there.

Quotation Content: There's a transition of fauna use that occurs early in site development. You have these big, wide, open mud flats which are excellent forging areas for shorebirds. During low tide shorebirds are just all over the mud flats, and they're chomping away. But as the site grades and channels develop and vegetation comes in, you lose some of that big open mud flat space right? But now you're transitioning from a habitat of like Shorebird foraging to Shorebird nesting. Because now you have, you get, it's basically all these channels. And then you have area available for endangered salt marsh harvest mice, for example. So now you're providing something more to the trophic cycle and habitat for mice and other things. Whereas before it's primarily shorebirds and then, when it's submerged, aquatic life. And speaking of transition to aquatic life, your fish use changes throughout that process. As your low grade, you get a lot more fish, you get a lot more large predatory fish coming in, and then as it transitions from a low, wide, mostly inundated area that's filled with fish to now you're figuring out something that's a little more channelized. Right? So now your fish are focused into the channels, and you might not provide as much in the context of invertebrates and fish food. So there's kind of a tradeoff that occurs, and it transitions through the development and through the process of the site evolving.
